Pepsin, Mucosal Injury, and Pathophysiology of Non-acid Reflux
  • Source: Otolaryngol Clin N Am 58 (2025) 415–432
  • Topic: Laryngopharyngeal reflux disease, pepsin-mediated airway injury, nonacid reflux pathophysiology
  • Author(s): Tina L. Samuels, MS; Nikki Johnston, PhD
1. Background: Reflux Disease Overview
  • GERD --> chronic gastrointestinal disorder characterized by regurgitation of gastric contents into the esophagus
  • LPR (laryngopharyngeal reflux) --> backflow of gastroduodenal contents into the laryngopharynx
  • Primary aggressors in reflux: acid, pepsin, and bile
  • Estimated 20% of population suffers from GERD and/or LPR
  • PPI (proton pump inhibitor) --> first-line therapy for GERD since acid-suppression success for peptic ulcer
1.1. PPI Limitations and Clinical Gaps
  • PPIs fail to resolve typical GERD symptoms in up to 40% of individuals
  • 2 decades of clinical trials fail to demonstrate placebo-adjusted benefit for LPR
  • Next-generation acid blockers (PCABs) show similar inadequacies for GERD/LPR symptom resolution
  • PPIs fail to protect against most life-threatening consequence --> carcinogenesis
  • Long-term high-adherence to PPI regimen associated with esophageal adenocarcinoma and high-grade dysplasia
  • Widespread PPI use since 1980s has not stemmed rising incidence of reflux-attributed cancers
1.2. Refluxate Composition Insights
  • Impedance-pH technology demonstrates: PPIs reduce acidity but do not reduce reflux frequency
  • Refluxate reaching laryngopharynx is commonly weakly to nonacidic
  • Bile present in some but not all refluxate
  • Only pepsin is present in ALL refluxate
  • Unconjugated bile acids causing damage at neutral-high pH are uncommon in refluxate
2. Reflux Disease: Inflammatory, Not Caustic Injury
  • Paradigm shift from "caustic injury" to "cytokine-mediated inflammation"
2.1. Historical Experimental Evidence
  • Early animal esophagi experiments (1960s) demonstrated: pepsin required for erosive lesions at pH of most esophageal reflux events
  • Pepsin essential to reflux-attributed injury even in injury-resistant esophagus
  • Subtle damage (epithelial barrier dysfunction) required pepsin or bile
2.2. Pepsin Properties and Behavior
  • Most enzymatically active at pH 2
  • Inactive at pH 6.5
  • Stable until irreversibly denatured at pH 8.0
  • Remains stable for ≥24 hours at pH 7
  • Regains up to 80% of original activity when returned to low pH
2.3. Pepsin Endocytosis Discovery (2007)
  • At pH 7, pepsin is endocytosed by laryngeal and hypopharyngeal epithelial cells
  • Stored in intracellular vesicles of pH 4–5 --> enzymatic activity restored
  • Intraepithelial retention persists for ≥36 hours
  • Confirmed in both laryngeal and esophageal cells
2.4. Cytokine-Mediated Injury Model
  • Surgical rodent model + GERD patients on 2-week PPI hiatus revealed:
  • Erosive lesions and barrier disruption require weeks to develop
  • Injury proceeds from submucosa to luminal surface (not inverse)
  • Sequence of events:
  • Epithelial production of ROS → secretion of proinflammatory cytokines
  • Lymphocytic infiltration of submucosa then epithelia
  • Widening of intercellular spaces
  • Basal cell and papillary hyperplasia → precedes surface erosions
3. Mechanisms of Peptic Injury During LPR
  • Pepsin may be enzymatically active or inactive (but stable) across pH range of LPR
  • Damage mechanisms differ with pH-dependent enzymatic activity
  • Acute inflammatory injury from brief pepsin exposure can contribute to fibrosis and carcinogenesis during unremitting LPR
3.1. Acute Inflammatory Injury by Nonacid Pepsin
  • Endocytosis is receptor-mediated
  • Putative receptor: low-density lipoprotein-related 1 (LRP1)
  • Pepsin may bind LRP-1 directly or via protease scavenger alpha-2 macroglobulin (a2M)
  • a2M binds proteases of all classes → entraps in cage-like structure → routes for endocytosis via LRP1
  • a2M-pepsin interaction occurs at pH 5.5 to 7 (weakly to nonacidic reflux)
3.1.1. ROS-Mediated Inflammation
  • Intracellular ROS elevation via NADPH oxidase (NOX) --> key initiating event
  • Early esophageal response: plasma membrane-situated NOX synthesizes ROS → expression of IL1-b and IL-8
  • IL-1b and IL-8 recruit immune cells → predict severity and subtype of disease
  • Pepsin in LPR patient laryngeal mucosa correlates with intracellular ROS and IL1-b
  • Single brief exposure to nonacid pepsin induces: ROS production + IL1-b expression + IL8 expression
  • Pretreatment with NOX inhibitor inhibits these effects
3.1.2. Immune Cell Recruitment
  • IL-8 --> strong chemoattractant of neutrophils
  • IL-8 recruits T-cells → establishes T-cell predominated response during early GERD stages
  • IL-8 is biomarker of GERD severity → elevated in erosive esophagitis → predicts symptomatic recurrence in non-erosive esophagitis
  • IL-8 and intraepithelial neutrophils correlate with disease severity during erosive and non-erosive esophagitis
  • Pepsin similarly promotes neutrophil invasion in airways
3.1.3. NLRP3 Inflammasome Activation
  • In laryngeal epithelia, pepsin-induced IL1-b and IL-8 expression via ROS is mediated in part by nucleotide-binding domain and leucine-rich repeat protein 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome
  • Multi-protein complex responds to ROS via caspase-mediated maturation of IL1-b precursor (pro-IL1-b)
  • NLRP3 implicated in pathogenesis of aerodigestive tract inflammatory disorders (e.g., intestinal inflammation, chronic obstructive airway disease) and carcinogenesis
  • Inhibition of NLRP3 partially reduced pepsin-induced cytokine expression → other mechanisms also contribute
3.1.4. HIF-2a Pathway
  • NOX-initiated and ROS-mediated depletion of prolyl hydroxylase (PHD) function
  • → promotes accumulation of hypoxia-inducible factor-2a (HIF-2a)
  • → subsequent NF-kB/p65-dependent cytokine expression during GERD
  • HIF-2a warrants investigation in LPR
3.1.5. Nociceptor Activation and Pain
  • Pepsin-induced ROS accumulation implicated in LPR symptom origination
  • Single 40-second exposure to weakly acid pepsin (pH 5) sensitized capsaicin-sensitive laryngeal afferent fibers
  • Result: laryngeal airway hypersensitivity (LAH) for ≥1 hour
  • Mechanism involves: ROS-induced ATP release → activation of P2X receptors
  • May be responsible for chronic cough during GERD and LPR (both coincident with reflux events and sensitization to irritants absent reflux event)
3.2. Acute Effects on Epithelial Barrier Integrity
  • Weakly acidified pepsin causes cleavage of adhesion molecule E-cadherin
  • Mechanism: regulated intramembrane proteolysis (RIP) not direct degradation
  • Pepsin (pH 4, 15 min) caused RIP of E-cadherin in laryngeal and esophageal cells in vitro
  • → dysregulation of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) associated with reflux-attributed cancers
  • RIP is evolutionarily conserved: 2 distinct enzymes perform sequential cleavage → peptide fragments persist in intracellular and extracellular space
  • E-cadherin RIP evidenced in GERD and LPR; also induced by acid via slower MMP-dependent mechanism over 24 hours
  • Pepsin induces epithelial monolayer barrier dysfunction within 15 minutes at pH 4
  • Can disrupt barrier function of differentiated tissue (porcine vocal fold, ex vivo) at pH 3 over 1 hour
  • Nonacid pepsin for up to 1 hour does not weaken monolayer barrier function; chronic nonacid pepsin exposure may do so secondary to inflammation
3.3. Chronic Nonacid Pepsin Exposure: Fibrosis and Carcinogenesis
  • Chronic inflammation promotes fibrosis
  • Pepsin observed in clinical specimens of idiopathic subglottic stenosis and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
  • Rat model of aspiration injury: nonacid pepsin elicits similar histologic grade of fibrosis and TGF-b (fibrosis-associated cytokine) expression as acidified pepsin
3.3.1. Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition (EMT)
  • Nonacid pepsin promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT)
  • EMT involved in fibrosis and cancer progression: epithelial cells lose polarity and adhesion → become migratory and invasive
  • Chronic pepsin exposure (0.1 mg/ml, 5 days) induced EMT of laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma cells
  • Markers: depletion of E-cadherin, elevation of mesenchymal markers (vimentin, b-catenin, snail, slug)
  • Effects: cell proliferation and migration
  • Partial mediation by IL-8 (attenuated via IL-8 receptor antagonist)
3.3.2. Carcinogenic Potential
  • Chronic inflammation contributes to tumorigenesis
  • Contrary to assumption: carcinogenic potential may be greater during weakly to nonacidic than acidic reflux
  • Extremely damaging nature of acidic pepsin (pH<4) rapidly reduces laryngopharyngeal cell viability
  • Chronic exposure to weakly to nonacid pepsin promotes: hyperproliferation, cell migration, anchorage independent growth, and cancer-associated gene expression changes
  • Pepsin associated with:
  • Tonsillar hypertrophy
  • Dysplastic grading and recurrence of vocal fold leukoplakia
  • Laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer and its nodal metastasis
3.3.3. DNA Damage Mechanisms
  • Unremitting high levels of ROS cause oxidative DNA damage → telomeric dysfunction and p53 mutation in Barrett's esophagus → progression to esophageal adenocarcinoma
  • Bile salts and acid induce 8-OH-dG and p-H2AX (markers of oxidative DNA damage and DNA double-strand breaks) in esophageal epithelial cells
  • Pepsin levels correlated with 8-OH-dG and p-H2AX in vocal fold polyps
  • Chronic nonacid pepsin exposure (6 hr/d, 5 d) induced 8-OH-dG, p-H2AX, and DNA fragmentation in laryngeal epithelial cells in vitro
3.3.4. NF-kB Signaling Pathway
  • NF-kB is nexus of inflammatory and carcinogenic molecular signaling
  • Often upregulated in head and neck squamous cell carcinomas (HNSCCs)
  • Promotes expression of anti-apoptotic factors (BCL2, STAT3, TNF-a) and cancer-associated cytokines (IL6, IL1b)
  • Forms positive feedback loop with epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), overexpressed in >80% of all HNSCCs
  • Activation of NF-kB by weakly and nonacid pepsin (pH 5–7) in airway epithelia widely reported
  • Chronic exposure to weakly or nonacidic pepsin (15 min/day, 5 days) led to activation of NF-kB, EGFR, STAT3
  • Upregulation of target genes: EGFR, AKT1, mTOR, IL1B, TNFA, RELA[p65], BCL2, IL6, STAT3
3.3.5. IL-8 in Tumorigenesis
  • IL-8 is important mediator of tumorigenesis and cancer progression during GERD
  • May play key role in pepsin-mediated tumorigenesis during LPR:
  • Pepsin and IL-8 correlated in laryngeal cancer
  • IL-8 receptor antagonist attenuated chronic pepsin-induced anchorage independent growth, cell proliferation, and migration of laryngeal epithelial cells (2 hr/d, 5 days)
4. Current Controversies and Diagnostic Challenges
4.1. Salivary Pepsin Diagnostic Utility
  • Meta-analyses conclude: diagnostic utility of salivary pepsin for LPR is low
  • No gold standard diagnostic for LPR to serve as reference
  • Valid pepsin assay and clinical sampling protocol not agreed upon
  • More sensitive than enzymatic assay, but reliability of antibody-based detection (ELISA, strip test) highly dependent upon antibody characteristics
  • Singular commercially available antibody-based salivary pepsin diagnostic device predominates among meta-analyses → inordinately influences study conclusions
4.2. Assay Specificity: Pepsin vs Pepsinogen
  • Pepsin produced as inactive pro-enzyme pepsinogen (373 amino acid protein)
  • Pepsinogen converted to active mature pepsin upon conformational change at low pH → removal of 47 amino acid N-terminal peptide
  • Human pepsinogen transcribed from PGA3, PGA4, and PGA5 on chromosome 11 → pepsinogens A3-5 with ~99% amino acid similarity
  • Abundantly transcribed in stomach, but low levels of pepsinogen synthesis occur in other anatomic sites including respiratory mucosa
  • PPIs may elevate gastric lumen pH above 5 for several hours daily → may reduce pepsinogen conversion (not adequately studied)
  • PPIs concentrate pepsin/pepsinogen in gastric juice by reducing acid secretion
  • Pepsinogen produced by other anatomic regions would rarely encounter low pH required for conversion to pepsin
  • Key distinction: pepsin abundant in refluxate (predominantly pepsin), very low levels pepsinogen may be locally synthesized in upper aerodigestive tract (predominantly pepsinogen)
4.3. Antibody Design Challenges
  • Pepsinogen A and pepsin amino acid sequences differ only by absence of 47 amino acid activation domains
  • Many commercial antibodies raised against full-length pepsin or pepsinogen → cannot discriminate between the 2
  • Insufficient detail provided for most commercial ELISAs to determine likelihood of discrimination
  • Rao and colleagues demonstrated: 3 commercial anti-pepsin antibodies and ELISA yielded positive signal in tissues/fluids that would not encounter refluxate (parotid gland, kidney, seminal fluid, urine)
  • Western blot revealed: only in esophagus was reactive band same molecular weight as stomach (likely pepsin - refluxed and endocytosed)
  • Band sizes commonly greater than pepsinogen (42 kDa)
  • LC-MS confirmed: 1 of 3 antibodies and ELISA produced signal in samples not found to contain pepsin or pepsinogen
  • LC-MS confirmed pepsin/pepsinogen in tissues beyond stomach, yet at levels several hundred times lower than in stomach
  • Endogenous pepsinogen production or serum pepsinogen may underlie reports of low levels of presumed pepsin detected by ELISA in tears of LPR patients
4.4. Detection of Rare Yet Disease-Reflux Events
  • Laryngopharynx exhibits much greater sensitivity to refluxate than esophagus
  • >1 hypopharyngeal reflux event in 24-h recording, regardless of pH, considered indicative of LPR
  • Refluxed pepsin present in saliva only transiently → high likelihood of false negative findings during 24-h monitoring despite multiple sampling
  • 36-h retention of pepsin by airway epithelia being leveraged to develop noninvasive cell-based assays (brushings) → superior sensitivity/specificity relative to saliva-based tests
5. Emerging Therapies: Pepsin Inhibition
5.1. Rationale for Targeting Pepsin
  • Only constituent found in ALL refluxate
  • Produced at several hundred-fold greater levels in stomach than other anatomic regions
  • Persists in airways of patients with LPR
  • Causes laryngeal inflammation, hypersensitivity, and cancer-related changes independent of gastric acid
5.2. Experimental Evidence for Pepsin Inhibitors
  • Enzymatic inactivation via pH > 8 or pharmacologic inhibitors attenuates all forms of experimentally induced peptic damage examined to date
  • Even effective in models of acid reflux (surgical or pH 3–4)
5.2.1. Pepstatin
  • Historical therapeutic development failed due to peptidic nature → poor bioavailability
  • Rescues injury in multiple models:
  • GERD rat model (surgical) → esophageal lesions
  • Porcine laryngeal mucosa (pepsin pH4)
  • Laryngeal epithelial cells (pepsin pH7) → IL1A, CSF2, EGR1, Hsp70 genes, mitochondrial morphology/function
  • Human tonsil mucosa ex vivo → CD4-positive cells, IL-2, IFN-g, IL-10
  • Laryngeal epithelial cells (pepsin pH7) → IL-6, IL-8 secretion, vimentin, b-catenin, E-cadherin protein level
  • Bronchial epithelial cells (pepsin pH3) → transepithelial neutrophil migration, HRP flux
5.2.2. HIV Protease Inhibitors
  • Recently proposed as novel pepsin-targeting therapeutics for reflux disease
  • Fosamprenavir and darunavir: oral gavage at manufacturer-recommended HIV/AIDS doses prevented histologic inflammatory injury in LPR mouse model (laryngeal scratch + topical pepsin)
  • Darunavir efficacy confirmed in surgical LPR model by objective measures: inflammation (wet weight, myeloperoxidase activity), mucosal integrity (transepithelial electrical resistance, fluorescein permeability)
  • In vitro data support capacity of active form of fosamprenavir (amprenavir) to inhibit peptic changes:
  • Epithelial barrier dysfunction in larynx and esophagus
  • Cancer-associated changes (E-cadherin cleavage, MMP gene expression)
  • Pilot epidemiologic data: lower prevalence of LPR among patients taking HIV protease inhibitors (0.2% vs 10%–34.4%, n=2062)
5.2.3. Formulation Development
  • Sustained-release oral fosamprenavir calcium with sodium alginate developed for proof-of-concept trial for LPR
  • Inhalation delivery ideal for respiratory symptoms associated with LPR:
  • Feasibility supported by efficacy of aerosolized fosamprenavir in LPR mouse model at 1/20th of oral dose
  • Inhaled fosamprenavir shows no adverse effects in pre-GLP toxicology assessment
  • Optimal particle size determined for laryngopharyngeal dry powder inhaler
5.3. Alginate-Based Therapies
  • Seaweed-derived alginate polysaccharides used for treatment of mild to moderate GERD
  • Clinical evidence supports benefit for GERD and LPR symptoms
  • Mechanisms:
  • Antioxidant properties → inhibition of pepsin
  • Mucoadhesive → forms raft over postprandial gastric acid pocket → reduces acidity (and presumably peptic activity) of refluxate
  • Gaviscon Double Action Liquid reduced acid reflux events for ≥2.5 hours postprandially
  • Topical mucoadhesive protection likely more brief
6. Future Considerations and Summary
6.1. Diagnostic Hurdles
  • Without advances in LPR diagnostics, cannot identify subjects whose symptoms are truly attributed to LPR (for whom pepsin inhibitor may provide benefit)
  • Evaluation of treatment efficacy confounded by inadequately validated clinical outcomes measures
  • FDA guidelines recommend patient-reported outcomes for therapeutic efficacy but establish more rigorous standards
  • No clinical outcomes measures currently qualify to evaluate efficacy of novel therapeutics for LPR → immense hurdle
6.2. Summary of Key Findings
  • Exclusively acid-targeting approach inadequate for reflux-attributed symptoms and carcinogenesis
  • Pepsin only weakly active/inactive in context of most LPR events, but:
  • Endocytosed and remains entrapped within airway mucosa long after reflux event
  • Activates many of same molecular mechanisms implicated in GERD pathogenesis
  • Sustained inflammatory response leads to fibrosis and cancer-associated changes
6.3. Clinical Care Points
  • Nonacid pepsin elicits inflammatory response → sustained → leads to fibrosis and cancer-associated changes in airway mucosa
  • Current medical therapies (PPI, PCAB) do not address nonacid pepsin
  • Acid-suppressing PPIs promote, rather than protect against, reflux-attributed carcinogenesis (likely due to continued exposure to nonacid pepsin)
  • Some alginate-antacid formulations inhibit pepsin and reduce LPR symptoms; provide temporary protection
  • Some FDA-approved HIV protease inhibitors are potent pepsin inhibitors → promise in preclinical studies
Appendix: Abbreviations
  • EMT: epithelial-mesenchymal transition
  • FDA: Food and Drug Administration
  • GERD: gastroesophageal reflux disease
  • HIF-2a: hypoxia-inducible factor-2a
  • HNSCC: head and neck squamous cell carcinomas
  • IL: interleukins
  • LAH: laryngeal airway hypersensitivity
  • LC-MS: liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
  • LPR: laryngopharyngeal reflux
  • LRP1: low-density lipoprotein receptor-related 1
  • MMP: matrix metalloproteinase
  • NLRP3: nucleotide-binding domain and leucine-rich repeat protein 3
  • PPI: proton pump inhibitor
  • RIP: regulated intramembrane proteolysis
  • ROS: reactive oxygen species
  • a2M: alpha-2 macroglobulin
